title: Module 5 quiz: Zero Trust and production patterns

questions.prompt: What's the core idea of Zero Trust?

choices.text: No implicit trust by network location. Every request is authenticated and authorized by identity + context (device, location, time), not by being 'inside the corporate network.' Replaces network-as-moat with identity-aware per-request access control.

questions.explanation: Identity, not network location, decides access. Compromised laptop on corporate wifi has no more access than a coffee-shop laptop.

questions.prompt: You want to expose a service running at home (behind NAT, ISP blocks port 80, no static IP). Right tool?

choices.text: Cloudflare Tunnel. cloudflared dials outbound; works through any NAT/firewall/ISP restriction. Five minutes of setup; no public IP; combines with Access for SSO gating. Replaces VPN for most internal-service access patterns.

questions.explanation: Tunnel's outbound-only design works through any NAT/firewall/ISP block. Combine with Access for identity gating. Replaces VPN for most teams.

questions.prompt: What's the canonical production pattern for most SaaS apps?

choices.text: All on Cloudflare or all on AWS.

Hybrid: Cloudflare for edge (DNS, security, caching, sometimes Workers/R2), cloud provider for compute and primary DB. Each for what it's best at. Often cheaper and more performant than all-one-provider.

questions.explanation: Hybrid is canonical. Cloudflare's edge strengths complement cloud's compute/data strengths. All-in on one provider often costs more for worse fit.

questions.prompt: Adding Cloudflare to an existing production setup. Safe approach?

choices.text: Flip everything to orange-cloud at once with defaults.

Gradual: DNS in Cloudflare with records gray-cloud first, SSL mode to Full (strict), Origin Cert installed, orange-cloud one record at a time with verification, WAF in Log mode initially, monitor for false positives, promote to Block over weeks, lock origin firewall to Cloudflare IPs once stable.

questions.explanation: Gradual migration limits blast radius. Big-bang flips routinely cause outages (wrong SSL mode, mail breakage, WAF blocking real users). Record-by-record + Log-mode + monitoring lets you catch issues at each step.

questions.prompt: Where do Cloudflare cost surprises typically come from?

choices.text: Viral Workers traffic exceeding included tier, R2 Class A/B operations at scale, D1 per-row read pricing under heavy reads, Workers AI inference, Logpush destination charges (Datadog etc.). DDoS is free; usage-based items are where bills sneak up. Set alerts on growth; review weekly.

questions.explanation: Cost surprises live in usage-based items, not fixed-tier features. DDoS, DNS, SSL are free. Workers, R2 ops, D1 reads, AI inference scale with usage. Alerts on growth catch surprises.
